slides.title: Hands-on tutorial: Using the Github web interface for authoring Internet-Drafts

chat.author: Alan DeKok
chat.text: NIT: page / chat title is still "eodir".

chat.author: Adrian Farrel
chat.text: This seems like a good place to say...

chat.author: Dhruv Dhody
chat.text: On data tracker this is still under eodir team activity

chat.author: David Lawrence
chat.text: AFAIK that's accurate and the way it typically is

chat.author: Adrian Farrel
chat.text: What dyall think about snark in the chats during WG meetings? The odd joke, maybe. Persistent pissy negativity, not so good. I have just witnessed newcomers going, "What? Really?" and the tone overflowing onto the mailing list.

chat.author: Karen O'Donoghue
chat.text: wgchairs forum is organized under eodir thus is shows up in datatracker that way. A bit confusing, yes. We've discussed changing it, but it hasn't risen to that level yet.

chat.author: Adrian Farrel
chat.text: I don't  think chairs can moitor the chat in real time while chairing. Do we need "jabber scribes" tto monitor and dampen? Do we need alternate venues for snark?

chat.author: Robert Sparks
chat.text: @Carsten - there's already a PR for datatracker that brings the reason shown on the queue page into the datatracker

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: Insert "second breakfast" meme pic here

chat.author: Robert Sparks
chat.text: For the "other" section of the AI discusson later: https://www.nostrum.com/~rjsparks/ietf126-drafts.html (done a few hours before the submission deadline ckosed, so if you try to check the work you might get slightly different numbers)

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: I feel like dropping AUTH48 rather than going back to its original definition is admitting defeat.

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: The goal has been defeated for several decades, so admitting it seems like a reasonable choice.

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: Yes, just lamenting that not holding people responsible for their work has won the day.

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: Well, we still hold them responsible, we just aren't able to hold them to deadlines when we're a volunteer organization.

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: "Build a system around it being unbounded" doesn't seem like a winning strategy either.

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: Oh, sure, there needs to be pressure to get things done.

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: I'm honestly not entirely sure whose responsibility it should be to apply that pressure.  The RFC Editor team?  The Document Shepherd?  The AD? The WG Chair?  That'd be a useful thing to nail down.

chat.author: Karen O'Donoghue
chat.text: Do you think the added visibility via the queue page can provide some of that pressure? Especially if that info is reflected back to datatracker (per Robert's earlier comment)

chat.author: Carsten Bormann
chat.text: My comment about "blocked" was specifically because I don't get to know sho to nudge.

chat.author: Carsten Bormann
chat.text: I'm not expecting that DT/RPC figure that out for me, but right now it is too hard.

chat.author: Éric Vyncke
chat.text: @Nick (Q is closed) https://github.com/evyncke/xml2docx has a XML to MD conversion tool that does the job at 99%

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: I think the move is to remind authors and chairs at the time it goes into the queue that they will have one brief window to weigh in on the final version, and we won't wait for them.  That's that.

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: Actually the shepherd writeup could include a "Confirm with the authors that they understand their AUTH48 obligations and the default" sorta deal.

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: So I guess I don't have a strong preference to where in the process we do that.  Maybe the problem is just that we assume people understand that obligation is there, and that assumption is false.

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: I think the corner case is that the RFC Editor needs someone to make a decision and that someone goes away indefinitely.

chat.author: Joe Clarke
chat.text: When I’ve acted as shepherd I check with the authors explicitly about AUTH48-like tasks.

chat.author: Kenneth Murchison
chat.text: I found the editors extremely responsive to feedback during AUTH48.  I had a few back and forth emails and my last RFC was published within the 48 hour window.  Authors just need to be on top of their emails from the editors.

chat.author: Jay Daley
chat.text: @Mirja - the current GitHub process was designed after two public sessions at IETF meetings and with extensive input from some heavy GH users, who signed off on the design as suitable for integrating RPC edits in the RPC repo into the authors repo.

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: "Feel free to encourage the authors to respond" doesn't work most of the time.  If I have to remind people, it's too late IMHO.

chat.author: Andrew Newton
chat.text: I agree. The kramdown-rfc docs need improvement.

chat.author: Justin Richer
chat.text: there are several conflicting pages in the repo

chat.author: Hans-Jörg Happel
chat.text: https://wiki.rfc.space/

chat.author: Justin Richer
chat.text: the README doesn't cover the same things as the wiki page

chat.author: Jay Daley
chat.text: At some point we need to make the decision on whether or not to have an IETF supported "official" markdown processor/variant.

chat.author: Justin Richer
chat.text: There's an unofficial template!

chat.author: Justin Richer
chat.text: like the unofficial markdown!

chat.author: Justin Richer
chat.text: (the unofficial tools are outrunning the official ones here)

chat.author: Alan DeKok
chat.text: If the RFC editor can provide stats on who's using word / xml / kramdown for inputs, that would tell us what people think is important.

chat.author: Justin Richer
chat.text: I'd be shocked if Word is > 0

chat.author: Chris Box
chat.text: @Jean yes, please publish the Rosetta Stone. (And just like in the British Museum, over time I expect thousands of people will come to view it)

chat.author: Mirja Kühlewind
Carsten Bormann
chat.text: (And it will help those of us who want to improve the documentation...)

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: Is this just an extension of IANABIS?

chat.author: Justin Richer
chat.text: what if the "spec" pointed to is a github file?

chat.author: Justin Richer
chat.text: (this is a real question)

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: Yep.

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: IETF Great Debate(tm)

chat.author: Sean Turner
chat.text: Internet-Draft is good 'nuff - see RFC 9847

chat.author: Mark Nottingham
chat.text: It feels like we're having an IANABIS session here

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: Yeah I said that above.

chat.author: Carsten Bormann
chat.text: Some IETFers seem to like a random github file better than an I-D.  The mind boggles.

chat.author: Mark Nottingham
chat.text: Considering this is a purportedly closed meeting, that seems wildly inappropriate

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text: Is "First Come First Served --> Approve everything" a DoS button for IANA registries? Can I set up a python script to request all codepoints?

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: @mnot++

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: @Carsten: Or even a specific commit of a particular file.

chat.author: Donald Eastlake
chat.text: It needs to always be clear that IANA can declare "abuse of process" and punt to the IESG.

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: I think a "please pay attention to ianabis" is a reasonable call for this group, but discussion should go onto that list (which nicely avoids the "conflcting meetings" problem).

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: Either.  A split discussion doesn't help the WG's outcome.

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text: So, then, process-wise: "First Come First Served --> Approve (mostly) everything" needs some strongly-worded footnotes about what counts as abuse? Sounds like a "I'll know it when I see it" thing that's hard to write policy for. DE does solve that.

chat.author: Carsten Bormann
chat.text: I think that "Spec required -- permissive" might be a great sub-policy for this, which we can use today (it is implemented by the DE).

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: Isn't that just "Spec Required" with permissive instructions to the DE?

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: Rather than being a sub-policy.

chat.author: Carsten Bormann
chat.text: Yes

chat.author: Carsten Bormann
chat.text: Having the sub-policy factored out simplifies the work for the author, but is not a necessary prerequisite.

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: I lean towards a smallish set of well-described policies with customization hooks where needed.

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: (customization is on the WG side, not the IANA side)

chat.author: Murray Kucherawy
chat.text: We want IANA's job to be made as easy and/or clear as possible.

chat.author: Carsten Bormann
chat.text: We have this problem that a IC policy is both IANA instruction and DE instruction -- these should actually be separate.

chat.author: Justin Richer
chat.text: amazing

chat.author: Renzo Navas
chat.text: could not get into the queue. I think we should add a section on genIA use disclosure (what for and how) at least on IDs. (even if to say that no GenIA has been used

chat.author: Renzo Navas
chat.text: ACM and IEEE made this compulsory for their academic papers

chat.author: Renzo Navas
chat.text: https://respect.acm.org/2026/index.php/policies-on-generative-ai-llms-and-related-tools/

chat.author: Renzo Navas
chat.text: https://open.ieee.org/author-guidelines-for-artificial-intelligence-ai-generated-text/

chat.author: Renzo Navas
chat.text:
IEEE "The use of content generated by artificial intelligence (AI) in an article (including but not limited to text, figures, images, and code) shall be disclosed in the acknowledgments section of any article submitted to an IEEE publication. The AI system used shall be identified, and specific sections of the article that use AI-generated content shall be identified and accompanied by a brief explanation regarding the level at which the AI system was used to generate the content.
The use of AI systems for editing and grammar enhancement is common practice and, as such, is generally outside the intent of the above policy. In this case, disclosure as noted above is recommended."

chat.author: Renzo Navas
chat.text: thank you
